element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player assigns statistics to chosen persona (Oda Nobunaga or rival daimyo)
Transfer resources between fiefs, manage taxes and productivity
Taxation decreases loyalty; food distribution and cultivation choices affect rebellion risk
Training increases fighting efficiency; units allocated to combat
Day-by-day turn resolution; victory through retreat, command destruction, or supply exhaustion
Long-term productivity gains with loyalty trade-offs
Time system where each turn = one year; leader mortality creates strategic vulnerability

typical_relief_triggers: Completing peasant morale recovery through food distribution
Securing economic stability through tax optimization
Passing turn with no immediate threats
typical_tension_triggers: Peasant loyalty dropping below threshold (rebellion risk)
Military supply exhaustion before battle conclusion
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Seasonal turn-based cycling with multi-layered simultaneous pressures: economic management, diplomatic positioning, and military preparation create cascading decision points where poor economic choices compound into military weakness and loyalty crises. Combat phases inject tactical intensity into strategic planning.

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Fiefs available for conquest; player can selectively engage with or ignore rival territories based on current strength

difficulty_curve.early_game: Tutorial-like resource allocation with minimal immediate threats; player learns economic and diplomatic systems without critical time pressure
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Rival daimyos begin aggressive expansion; player must balance economic development with military preparation; combat encounters escalate in frequency and scale
difficulty_curve.late_game: Daimyo ages toward mortality; late-game competitors have consolidated power; unification victory requires sustained military dominance and economic efficiency
notable_spikes: First major military confrontation (transition from economic to combat focus)
Multiple simultaneous rival threats (requires strategic prioritization)
Final unification campaigns (resource drain from sustained warfare)

notable_level_patterns.description: Four scenarios with increasing starting dates (1560→1582) mirror increasing mid-game complexity; earlier scenarios allow longer preparation; later scenarios compress timeline toward unification deadline
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Battle for the East (1560) vs. Road Towards Unification (1582)

notable_level_patterns.description: Each turn requires deciding between investment in productivity/loyalty (peace resources) or military/recruitment (war resources); forces recurrent tension between competing strategic priorities
Strategic campaign map transitions to tactical 5x10 hex grid during battles; day-by-day resolution extends single combat decision into multi-turn commitment

sources_used: wikipedia: Nobunaga's Ambition (video game) - Gameplay section, Reception section
